title: Understand: Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights: Part I
description: Check your understanding of the modern debates over elections and voting rights with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights: Part I
Understand: Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights: Part I

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Twenty-Sixth Amendment, passed in 1971, banned voting discrimination on the basis of what?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of these advancements were accomplished by the passage of a constitutional amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The banning of poll taxes in national elections

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Twenty-Sixth Amendment, ratified in 1971, established a national voting age of \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which section of the Fourteenth Amendment explicitly mentions voting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**In *Harper v. Virginia State Board of Elections,* the Supreme Court ruled that poll taxes violated which part of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**What is the significance of the Supreme Court’s holding in *Harper v. Virginia State Board of Elections*? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It ended poll taxes in state and local elections.
It gave the federal government power to monitor election practices.
It eliminated all barriers to voting in southern states.
It affirmed that states have the power to establish the manner of elections.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these are examples of “manner” regulations that Congress can alter? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which statement best describes how the modern Supreme Court has interpreted the Equal Protection Clause, as it relates to the federal government’s power over elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gave the federal government more power to oversee and regulate the manner of elections.
It expressly outlawed federal interference with elections, as only states have the power to administer elections.
It gave the federal government more power to oversee the formation of congressional districts, but nothing else.
It weakened the Tenth Amendment and gave the responsibility of regulating elections entirely to the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does the phrase “one person, one vote” mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Districts need to have roughly equal numbers of people.
Each person can vote only once in federal elections.
All members of Congress must be elected at the same time.
The electoral votes must match the popular vote.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which Supreme Court cases helped uphold the idea of “one person, one vote”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Harper v. Virginia State Board of Elections*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes the Supreme Court’s decision in *Reynolds v. Sims*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Unequal voting districts violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Poll taxes violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
The Voting Rights Act of 1965 is a valid exercise of Congress’s power to enforce the Fifteenth Amendment.
States have the power to restrict a person’s right to vote if they have been convicted of a felony.
